raw_consumer_statement:
In XX/XX/XXXX, I was the victim of a phishing scam involving impersonation of the U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission ( EEOC ). My business received what appeared to be a legitimate request for quotation and purchase order ( XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX ) from someone identifying as a federal procurement officer, using the spoofed email address XXXX. 

Believing this was a legitimate federal contract, I used my American Express card to purchase equipment totaling {$40000.00}, including {$36000.00} from XXXX on XX/XX/XXXX and three XXXX shipments totaling {$4100.00} on XX/XX/XXXX and XX/XX/XXXX. The shipment address was later discovered to be a retail strip mall in XXXX, XXXXnot a federal facility.

After shipment, I identified several red flags ( including a fraudulent domain, fake documents, and a non-government delivery location ), and I reported the fraud to Amex. My claim was denied on the grounds that I had voluntarily authorized the charges.

However, I made the purchases under criminal deception. This is a classic example of authorized fraud caused by impersonation and social engineering.

I have since filed reports with : The FBI IC3 ( complaint filed XX/XX/XXXX ) The FTC ( complaint filed XX/XX/XXXX ) The XXXX XXXX Police Department ( Report # XXXX ) I am requesting that American Express reopen and properly investigate this case in light of the full documentation and law enforcement reports, and that the charges be reversed under fraud protection policies.
